7.1: I am bereft! I am like a gleaner in a stripped vineyard — searching for one cluster, one first-ripe fig, and finding nothing. What my whole self longed for has been harvested away.
7.2: The devout are gone from the earth — not one honest soul remains. Every person is hunting the next, lying in ambush, casting nets for blood. Brotherhood has become predation.
7.3: They have mastered the craft of corruption: the official names his price, the judge names his reward, the powerful simply state what they want — and together they weave it all into policy.
7.4: Even their best people draw blood like a briar; even their most upright tear like thornbush. The day the prophets forewarned has come — judgment has arrived — and now the wicked stagger in confusion.
7.5: Trust no one — not your neighbor, not your closest confidant. Even the words spoken in your own bed must be guarded. Intimacy itself has become dangerous.
7.6: The household itself has fractured: son against father, daughter against mother, daughter-in-law against mother-in-law. Your worst enemies sleep under your own roof.
7.7: But I — I will fix my eyes on Yahweh. I will wait, however long it takes, for the God who saves. He will hear me.
7.8: Do not celebrate my fall, enemy. I have gone down — but I will rise. I sit in the dark now; but the LORD himself is my light.
7.9: I will accept Yahweh's wrath — I have earned it by my sin — and I will wait until he rises to argue my case and render his verdict. He will bring me into the light; I will see his vindication with my own eyes.
7.10: Then my enemy will see it — the one who sneered, 'Where is your God now?' Shame will cover her face. I will watch it happen with my own eyes: she who mocked will be trampled into the mud of the streets.
7.11: A day is coming to rebuild your walls. On that day every ordinance that constrained you will be swept away, and your borders will stretch to their widest.
7.12: On that day the scattered will come home — from Assyria, from Egypt, from the great River to the farthest sea, from mountain range to mountain range — streaming back to you from every direction.
7.13: But first the earth must be stripped bare — a desolation earned by those who live there, the bitter harvest of what they have sown.
7.14: Take up your shepherd's staff, O LORD, and lead your people — the flock that is yours alone. They live now like creatures hidden in woodland at the edge of a garden. Lead them out to graze freely in Bashan and Gilead again, as they did in the days of old.
7.15: I will do for them what I did when you marched out of Egypt. New wonders, just as astonishing, for a new generation to witness.
7.16: The nations will watch what God does — and all their vaunted power will wilt into shame. They will cover their mouths in stunned silence; they will be deaf to the sound of their own boasting.
7.17: They will lick the dust like serpents, crawling from their strongholds in terror. Nations that once fielded armies will come out trembling, cringing before Yahweh our God — all their former pride collapsed into fear.
7.18: Who is there like you, O God? You pardon our iniquity; you pass over rebellion — for the sake of those who remain as your inheritance. Your anger has an end because your deepest delight is the love that never lets go.
7.19: He will have compassion again — compassion that circles back even to the guilty. He will crush our wrongdoing beneath his feet. Every sin we have ever committed will be hurled into the deepest abyss of the sea, as he once hurled Pharaoh's army into the deep, never to return.
7.20: You will keep the oath — faithfulness to Jacob, covenant love to Abraham — the very promises sworn to our ancestors in the ancient days. The God who made those vows still stands by every one of them.

1.1: Micah of Moresheth received the LORD's word in the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah over Judah — a vision whose targets were Samaria and Jerusalem.
1.2:
Every nation, listen!
All the earth and all its fullness — hear the charge!
The Sovereign LORD himself is the witness against you,
the Lord who speaks from his holy sanctuary.
1.3:
Watch — Yahweh is departing from his place!
He descends; he treads across the heights of the earth.
No mountain is high enough to stop him.
1.5:
All this fury — on account of Jacob's rebellion,
the sins of the house of Israel.
Who is the source of Jacob's rebellion? Samaria.
Who erected Judah's idolatrous shrines? Jerusalem.
1.6:
So Samaria becomes a heap of rubble on an open hillside,
a site cleared for vineyards.
I will tumble her stones down into the ravine
and strip her down to bedrock.
1.7:
Her idols are smashed, every carved image shattered;
the shrine-wages will burn in the fire.
All her gods I will ruin.
She built her cult from the earnings of harlotry —
to harlotry's earnings they will return.
1.8:
So I am left to wail and howl,
to walk stripped bare and naked.
I cry like a jackal;
I mourn like an owl in the desert night.
1.10:
Say nothing in Gath — do not give Philistia the news.
Weep instead, but silently.
In Dust-town — Beth-le-aphrah — roll in the dust of mourning.
1.11:
Fairtown — Shaphir — march into exile, naked and ashamed.
Exodus-town — Zaanan — cannot march out to help.
Beth-ezel is in ruins: the place you leaned on has no ground left to stand on.
1.12:
Bitterness-town — Maroth — waits and hopes for good news.
None comes: Yahweh himself has sent the disaster
all the way to Jerusalem's gate.
1.13:
Harness the horses, Lachish — your war chariots will not save you!
You were the first to import Israel's sin into Judah,
the gateway through which the northern rebellion entered Zion.
1.14:
So bid farewell with parting gifts to Moresheth-gath —
you are losing her forever.
And Achzib? Its name means Deception:
that is all the kings of Israel will get from it.
1.15:
Mareshah — Heir-town — gets a foreign heir:
a conqueror is coming to possess you.
The glory of Israel will find its refuge
in the cave of Adullam — a fugitive king once more.

2.1:
Woe to those who lie awake at night scheming evil,
plotting injustice when they should be sleeping.
At sunrise they act — because they have the power to.
2.2:
They see a field and they want it — so they take it.
They see a house and they want it — so they grab it.
They crush a man, strip away his household,
rob him of the land his fathers left him.
2.3:
Therefore the LORD has this to say:
I too am plotting — plotting disaster for this family.
You will not pull your necks out of that yoke.
No more walking about with heads held high —
this is going to be a time of catastrophe.
2.4:
When that day comes, a mocking dirge will be sung against you —
a bitter requiem:
'We are finished — ruined to the last!
Our land has been handed to strangers;
what was ours has been stripped away.
Our fields are divided out to our conquerors.'
2.5:
Because of this, not one of you will stand in the LORD's assembly
to cast the lot for the land.
The inheritance is forfeited.
2.6:
'Shut up!' they say — they the preachers!
'Don't prophesy like this; don't bring such shame on us!'
But disgrace is already on its way.
2.7:
People of Jacob, is this the right question to be asking?
Is the Spirit of the LORD limited or impatient?
Would God actually do such things?
Only ask whether you are walking uprightly —
because my words do good to those who are.
2.8:
But my own people have turned predator:
you strip the cloaks off travelers who pass in good faith —
men coming home from battle, wanting nothing but peace —
and you ambush them.
2.9:
You evict widows from the homes they love.
You rob their children of everything I intended for them —
not just property, but dignity, inheritance, a future.
2.11:
The only prophet this people wants
is one who lies with breath and bluster and says:
'Wine! Strong drink! That is what I see in your future!'
That kind of prophet they will follow.
2.12:
But I will gather you, Jacob — every last one.
I will bring the remnant of Israel home,
set them like sheep in a pen,
like a flock in the middle of good pasture.
The noise of many people — coming home.
2.13:
The Breaker goes ahead of them —
he smashes a path through the gate.
They pour through it, streaming into the open.
Their king at the front; Yahweh himself leading the way.

3.1:
Then I said:
Listen, you leaders of Jacob,
you rulers who govern the house of Israel —
is it not your whole charge to know what justice is?
3.2:
You who despise good and embrace evil —
who skin my people alive,
who strip the flesh from their bones —
3.3:
who devour my people's flesh,
tear off their skin,
crack their bones,
chop them into pieces for the cooking pot,
like chunks of meat in a boiling cauldron.
3.4:
When judgment falls, they will cry out to the LORD —
and he will not answer.
He will hide his face from them then,
because of the evil they have practised.
3.5:
This is what the LORD says against the prophets
who mislead my people:
if you feed them, they cry 'All is well!'
If you do not, they call down war against you.
They sell their oracles for a meal.
3.6:
So your light goes out —
no more visions, no more dreams.
Darkness for the seers;
the sun sets at noon for the prophets,
and the day turns black.
3.7:
The seers will be shamed.
The diviners will cringe.
Every last one of them will put their hands over their mouths —
because God will not speak to them.
3.8:
But I am different:
filled with power by the Spirit of the LORD,
full of justice and courage —
to name Jacob's rebellion for what it is
and tell Israel the truth about its sin.
3.9:
Hear this, rulers of Jacob,
leaders of Israel:
you who detest justice,
who twist everything straight into a tangle —
3.10:
who erect Zion's walls with the blood of the poor
and raise Jerusalem's buildings on a foundation of injustice.
3.11:
The judges take bribes.
The priests sell their rulings.
The prophets peddle their visions for a fee —
and then they all lean on the LORD:
'God is with us! Nothing bad will happen here!'
3.12:
Therefore — because of you —
Zion will be plowed under like a field.
Jerusalem will become a rubble heap.
The hill where the temple stands will go back to forest.
[This oracle was cited verbatim in Jer 26:18 as precedent for Jeremiah's temple sermon.]

4.1:
But in the days to come
the mountain of the LORD's house
will stand at the summit of all mountains,
lifted high above every hill.
Nations will come streaming toward it.
[This vision is shared almost word-for-word with Isaiah 2:2–4.]
4.2:
Many nations will flow in:
'Come — let us go up to the LORD's mountain,
to the house of Jacob's God.
Let him show us how to live;
let us walk his road.'
For from Zion the Torah goes forth,
and from Jerusalem comes the word of the LORD.
4.3:
He will rule between peoples, far and near;
nations will lay their quarrels before him.
Then:
swords beaten into plowshares,
spears into pruning hooks.
Nation will not draw a sword against nation;
no one will study war anymore.
4.4:
And every person will sit
under their own vine, their own fig tree,
with no one left to threaten them —
because the LORD of hosts has spoken it.
4.5:
Every nation still follows its own gods —
but we will walk in the name of the LORD our God,
now and always.
4.6:
In that coming day — Yahweh's own word —
I will gather the lame,
I will bring home the scattered,
all those I struck down in my anger.
4.7:
The lame will become the remnant;
the exiles will become a powerful nation.
The LORD will be king over them on Mount Zion
from that day forever.
4.8:
And you, watchtower of the flock,
fortress hill of daughter Zion —
the old sovereignty is coming back to you,
the kingdom for daughter Jerusalem.
4.9:
Then why are you crying out now?
Have you no king? Has your wise counsel collapsed?
Is that why pain has gripped you like a woman in labor?
4.10:
Writhe in your labor pain, daughter Zion —
you are going through what a mother goes through.
You will leave the city; you will live in the open.
You will go all the way to Babylon.
But there — there! — Yahweh will rescue you;
he will redeem you from your captors' hands.
4.11:
Now the nations are massing against you,
saying: 'Desecrate her!
Let us enjoy the spectacle of Zion's ruin!'
4.12:
They do not know what the LORD is thinking;
they cannot read his counsel.
He is gathering them — the way a farmer gathers sheaves —
straight to the threshing floor.
4.13:
Rise up, daughter Zion — thresh them!
I am making your horns iron,
your hooves bronze.
You will pulverize many nations.
Their wealth is consecrated to Yahweh,
their spoil to the Lord of all the earth.

5.1:
Muster your forces, besieged city!
The enemy has surrounded us;
they humiliate our ruler —
struck on the cheek with a rod.
5.2:
But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah —
too small to appear on the clan-list of Judah —
from you will come the one who is to govern Israel for me.
His origins reach back before memory's beginning,
to ages before time.
5.3:
And so for now he abandons them —
until the birth: until the labor is over.
Then the exiled brothers will return
to join the people of Israel.
5.4:
He will stand firm and tend his people
in the strength of the LORD,
in the authority of the LORD his God's name.
They will settle in safety —
for his greatness will reach to the ends of the earth.
5.5:
This ruler will himself be peace.
When Assyria invades — when they march through our open doors —
we will set against them seven shepherds, eight commanders:
an overwhelming response.
5.6:
They will take the sword to Assyria itself,
conquer Nimrod's ancient land from its own gates.
He will rescue us from the Assyrian invasion —
every foot of ground within our borders.
5.7:
The remnant of Jacob scattered among the nations —
like dew from the LORD,
like sudden showers on the grass:
unaided by human effort,
given by God alone.
5.8:
The remnant of Jacob among the peoples —
like a lion in the forest,
like a young lion loose among sheep:
it passes through; it tears; it takes;
and no one can stop it.
5.9:
Your hand will be raised against every enemy;
all who oppose you will be swept away.
5.10:
In that coming day — Yahweh declares —
I will strip away your warhorses,
smash your chariots to pieces.
5.11:
I will demolish your fortified cities,
pull down every stronghold where you felt secure.
5.12:
I will cut the sorcery from your hands;
no more oracle-merchants in your land.
5.14:
I will tear out your Asherah poles
and raze your cities to the ground.
5.15:
And the nations that refused to listen —
I will deal with them in fury,
in the heat of my wrath.

6.1:
Listen to what the LORD is saying:
Rise up — bring your case before the mountains.
Let the hills be your audience.
6.3:
'My people — what have I done to you?
Tell me how I have burdened you.
Answer me! Make your case.'
6.4:
'I brought you out of Egypt.
I redeemed you from the slave house.
I gave you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam to lead the way.'
6.5:
'Remember Balak's scheme, king of Moab —
and how Balaam son of Beor answered him.
Remember the journey from Shittim to Gilgal —
every step of it is Yahweh's saving righteousness toward you.'
6.6:
'How can I approach the LORD?
How do I bow before the high God?
Shall I bring burnt offerings?
Year-old calves? Is that the price?'
6.7:
'Thousands of rams — ten thousand streams of olive oil —
would that be enough?
Shall I give my own child,
my firstborn, for my crimes?
The fruit of my own body for the sins of my life?'
6.9:
The LORD's voice calls to the city.
Wisdom means fearing that name.
Listen — all you clans, all you citizens:
who appointed the rod that is coming?
6.10:
'The wicked house still packed with ill-gotten wealth;
the dishonest measure — cursed, and still in use.'
6.11:
'Can I call that person innocent
who cheats with rigged scales
and a pouch of false weights?'
6.13:
'Therefore I am striking you down —
an incurable blow;
I am making you waste away
because of what you have done.'
6.14:
'You will eat — and still be empty.
You will store up — and lose it.
Whatever you manage to hold on to,
I will hand over to the sword.'
6.15:
'You will plant — but not harvest.
You will press the olives — but never rub on the oil.
You will crush the grapes — but never taste the wine.'
